title: CEA Economic Report of the President 2026 - Treasury Yield Curves
content: Chapter 14 of the 2026 Economic Report of the President tracks the U.S. term structure of interest rates and reports both the nominal Treasury yield curve and the yield curve inferred from Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities, usually called TIPS. Plotting nominal and TIPS curves at year-end allows the Report to decompose movements in interest rates into changes in expected real rates and changes in expected inflation. The CEA notes that during episodes like the COVID-19-induced recession, changes to the nominal yield curve were parallel to those for the real yield curve inferred from TIPS, suggesting that movements in these interest rates predominately reflected adjustments to expected real interest rates. The Report also tracks key short-term rates, including the effective federal funds rate and the 3-month Treasury rate, alongside the 10-year Treasury rate, so that the level and shape of the curve can be read together with monetary policy stance. Gray bars in the Report's yield-curve figures mark NBER-dated recessions, anchoring the term-structure discussion in the official U.S. business-cycle chronology. The framework implies that the spread between long- and short-maturity Treasury yields is read as a forward-looking signal about expected real rates and expected inflation, and that a flattening or inverted curve is interpreted together with the policy rate path and the real-rate decomposition, not mechanically. Analysts watching the 10-year-minus-2-year Treasury spread can apply the same reading: check the nominal curve and the TIPS curve side by side, anchor the level against the federal funds rate, and overlay NBER recession bars to see how the current shape compares with past cycles.
